sentences.text: We were--
I don't think we looked at the clock.
They didn't-- sometimes we had to make deliveries.
And one trip up to the mountain, they even told us-- they called-- the name of the mountain, for example, was [?
Rockita ?].
Rockita ?] they said it's about 28 hours walking up. 28 hours to walk up on a mountain is more than a day's constantly walking.
In the meantime, there were such things like air raid, because the Russians were-- but there was no such thing like-- normally it was supposed to be from 6:00 in the morning until 7:00 at night.
But under-- when you are in war, then all these rules don't count too much.
Naturally the Russians were stronger and we knew that.
And we hoped they were going to make a fast job.
Unfortunately, it took much longer than it should.
We were retreating.
And this retreating business wasn't such a thing like we were taking the truck or a train coming back, but we were marching back everything.
Everything walking.
Everything on its own with our packages.
And in many cases, we were carrying heavy tools, farmer tools, [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know how to-- how to call that.
Big, what you-- people who are breaking stones, you see those--
Ax.
That was-- as a matter of fact, we had even a-- a song about the-- about this forced laborers in Hungarian, [NON-ENGLISH].
This was our-- these were our tools.
We were marching back.
And on the way back, this was probably around the high holidays times, must have been close to October.
We were-- we were still out on the Russian front when it was Yom Kippur.
And remember that we had to burn beautiful wooden buildings.
By coming home we says that probably they had found the Hungarians, that they ask us to burn as many buildings as-- as we can.
They probably put on some gasoline [INAUDIBLE].
And we were marching and burning, burning and marching, and-- and this was even on the holiday of Yom Kippur we were doing it, coming back from Osmoloda, back toward Hungary.
This was in the Carpathian area which is called Ruthenia.
Today it's Ruthenia, belongs today to Ukraine.
That was the time when we did hear-- we were in a town, it's called Huszt, probably.
October 15th we heard on the big news that the Hungarian regent, Admiral Nicholas Horthy, gave a notice on the radio that he is looking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for peace.
He doesn't want to continue the war.
Naturally, he was foolish enough to let the Germans know what he wants to do.
And the Germans just picked him up and took him away.
And this big holiday what we expected that the next day the whole thing will be over fizzled out.
Unfortunately, it didn't happen.
But instead of Horthy, the Germans took over.
Veesenmayer was the German consulate-- consul general.
Actually he was the-- Hitler's main representative in Hungary.
And he was already dictating what the Hungarian government should do.
They appointed this biggest dog what Hungary had was Szalasi, this most brutal animal who was-- who took over the government.
The most brutal way he was, he and his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] group, which was called the-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH], but they have a word for it.
They had the arrow, the arrow-- they had the--
Arrow Cross.
They were the most sadistic youngsters you can imagine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were ready to go into a hospital and put everything on fire.
You had hundreds of people, children or women, doesn't matter.
It didn't mean nothing.
As long as they know that they were Jewish, they were taking out people.
Doctors, they took out 30-- 300 doctors they took out into the Danube and they were just killing them.
The water of the Danube was red for days, people were saying.
My own brother was in Budapest and he witnessed this abnormal brutality what this Szalasi and his gangsters committed.
Anyway, we were out in-- in Ruthenia, marching back to Hungary.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we didn't know about what Szalasi was doing in Budapest, but the war continued.
We knew that it wasn't what we-- we expected to be.
And we were marching back from-- from the border all the way, little by little by little, up to the Miskolc through [NON-ENGLISH], which was a well known town in Hungary.
On the roads, they were full of beautiful-- it was in the time of the year when they had beautiful grapes and all the fruit, fruit orchards were full.
And there was-- we were not hungry there.
We had plenty to eat in these Hungarian towns.
And we spoke their language.
It was a different game than outside.
Sure enough, we were coming in to the Miskolc area.
We stayed there for about two weeks in that place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But Hungary was little by little occupied by the Germans-- by the Russians.
And all those labor camps or forced laborers, the Germans and the Hungarian government did not want us to fall into the hands of the Russians, because that would mean liberation for us.
And they didn't want us to be liberated.
They wanted-- they wanted us to be wiped out or their slave.
So they were marching everybody toward the west.
How do you get to the west from Kosice?
So what they did, they took us in to Kosice, Kosice, which is today the second largest town in Slovakia.
It was a beautiful Jewish community many, many years ago.
I was very close with that town, because I had uncles there and I had family there.
They took us with the-- with-- with-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Trucks?
--With trucks.
They took us with trucks into Slovakia, into a town Margarita, Margarita.

And from Margarita we were taken into train, to a big cattle trains, through whole Slovakia, from the east to the west, up to the Hungarian-Austrian border, which is called-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was called-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Well, I-- I wounded up in a town which is called Balf.
But it was [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was probably the station.
What they did, these monsters, that they took two carloads in one little town, two carloads in another little town.
You had brothers in one and another brother there.
They couldn't give a-- couldn't care less.
So we wounded up in a town which is called Balf, B-A-L-F.
It's not far from Sopron.
Sopron is a big town on the Hungarian-Austrian border.
There's a beautiful lake over there.
It's called Ferto.
And we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taken over by Germans.
And this town was already full of German-speaking Hungarians which is called Swabs.
And they were pretty, pretty tough with anti-Semites.
We were taken into-- every house had in the back a stable where the cows or the horses or-- or plain straw was.
So that was our-- that was our apartment.
And it was good, because it was not as cold.
But the winter came.
This was Hanukkah time.
And we had to go out every day-- every day to-- to the lake, to work in the lake.
The lake was deep.
And they were-- and they gave us rubber shoes, which was half the size-- half of the depth of the water, so it didn't mean too much.
Hundreds of people came down with fleck typhus.
And that was the first time we did see people with fleck typhus.
We couldn't figure out what it was that all of a sudden people were-- they were just-- they were just talking-- they were just talking like mish-mash, completely.
They had such high temperature-- Excuse me.
They were totally, totally sick.
Whole night they were talking and we didn't-- we didn't know what it was.
This is the first time we saw what it means, fleck typhus.
And in two or three days later, they were just-- they passed out.
So they had over there a whole group of organized Jewish funeral groups.
Every day they were taking a blanket.
And now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mr.
Weiss and now Mr.
Weinstein and now Mr.
Zoly.
People were just dying.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And in this-- under-- in this town of Balf, I was together with three of my friends who were 16-, 17-year-old boys.
And I was very, very close with them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you couldn't stay home for work unless you had a paper from the doctor that you are not well.
If you are not well, you can stay home tomorrow.
And tomorrow morning, a young punk, 16-year-old kids came in with a big rifle.

And if you didn't see-- if he didn't see the paper from the doctor, they were just taking you out and shoot you. 16-, 17-year-old kids, they had the right to kill like if I would step-- I would think twice before I step on a-- on a-- on a bug.
It was such a simple thing for them to-- to kill somebody.
Once it happened to me-- I'll never forget that.
We arrived over there and that time, this place, no more Jewish armband, but the Jewish star.
They gave us star, Jude.
It was something-- we didn't know.
And I put it on on my winter coat.
And I took off my winter coat, I went out for water without my Jude star.
And a young monstrous kid comes over to me.
And he wants to-- takes me and ready to shoot me, because I didn't wear my star.
And there was a Mr.
Rozenfeld, who was about my father's age.
He came out the back to him.
He says, we just arrived.
We didn't know the rules.
Give him a chance.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's how-- and this man, Mr.
Rozenfeld, he was killed.
But he saved my life, because this young punk was ready to kill me for not putting on the coat which had the Jewish star on.
And this was a few minutes after we arrived in this place.
We stayed there few weeks, working there.
And I got my toes frozen in this place, because we're going out to the lake, coming home.
And one of my shoes was more tight than the other.
I got it from somebody.
A good-- a good man gave it to me, Joshua.
His name was Greenfeld.
He was in a high position and he gave me a pair of army shoes.
But it wasn't a pair.
And the left was one-- the left one was a little bit smaller.
And this is a good thing to learn, a lesson.
If your shoes are tight, it's very easy to freeze, because there's no air to keep it warm.
And up to the present day, I'm suffering from three toes.
I had cellulitis about 25 times because of these frozen toes-- my left-- on my left foot.
They're twice the size of the normal-- of the other ones.
And because of these frozen toes, I was given a vacation.
I didn't have to go out.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from every-- every-- every bad part has some positive in it too.
And what I was doing was a dangerous thing, but I did it.
I had a ticket to stay home.
And middle of the day, I was hiding two of my friends under my blanket, because it was cold.
In daytime, you stay inside.
You're not supposed to go out.
And then, those young kids came with their gun to see that I have a paper.
I have two boys hiding under my blanket.
If they would-- if they would just go like this, we would all be killed.
This was 'til a few weeks before Passover this was going on.
And all of a sudden my doctor-- the same doctor who was-- who took care of my frozen toe, he says, you know what?
I think you should leave that place already, because this place was full of people who were sick with typhus.
And you're much better off to go out from there.
And I told him, but I have my friends there.
And this doctor took me out.
And I became his-- what you call?-- a little servant to him.
Helper?
Helper.
Somehow he liked me.
I had-- and I had a little bit more-- more to eat than the rest.
And I took-- every day, I took carrot soup to these friends of mine into the fleck typhus place.
And a few days later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they told us we have to march further, because the Germans are moving back.
The Russians are attacking.
And there's no more room for us to stay.
However, all those Jewish colleagues of ours who were in the fleck typhus barracks-- barracks-- they stay here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What happened to them, do I have to tell you?
The last Germans took them out, all of them, and they-- in the same ditch, they-- they wiped them out.
This Mr.
Rozenfeld was one of them, who saved my life, was killed in that day.
This is the second day of Passover or first day of Passover.
This was in the early part of '45?
Early-- early part of '45.
And we were marching out of Balf, B-A-L-F, toward more into Austria, because this was already-- how much?
This was the last three months of the war.
But it was the most horrible few weeks, from Passover to the end of the war.
It was the most horrible week-- weeks.
How shall I say it?
The trip, the march, this so-called death march, which took place from before Passover 'til we got to Mauthausen, there are books about this and there were many articles about this.
They called it the death march in the upper Alps.
I saw it in a Hungarian newspaper.
It was something which is-- which is-- you need to be a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lion to-- even to talk about it.
It was very, very hard days.
These boys and Mr.-- my friends, they were killed two days after that.
How do I know it?
Because there is one man here living in Brooklyn.
His name is Mr.
Vitriol.
He was among them.
And he fall down into the ditch.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he got probably one shot in his leg, but he lost his consciousness.
And the rest of them were falling on top of them.
And a few hours later, the Russians came and says, [NON-ENGLISH], this one is still alive.
And they pulled him out.
The man is living here in Borough Park.
He is a old man today and a sick man.
But he was one of the very miraculously [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] saved person.
His name is Mr.
Vitriol, a very, very nice, well learned man.
And he was telling us how that happened, that the very last Germans, they went into the-- to these barracks, because as soon as we left, they nailed these barracks.
The-- the Germans nailed them that these-- these-- these Jewish boys, they shouldn't be able to go out.
And the very last one, they call it the Sonderkommando, they were specializing in this.
They came in and they chased them out.
One of them I know put on his tallis.
He put on his tallis.
He knew what's coming.
His name was Lebovich.
And they went out to the same ditch what we were digging the whole few weeks there.
And in the same ditch, they were buried.
They were-- they were-- they were executed.
Among them was I would say about two dozen were very, very close friend of-- friends of mine from my town.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was in Balf.
One-- one of them was a-- one man, who I met in America, his son was there and he went back.
And because those days, they still-- we still put on a chain or a name for each of these deceased one.
Those people who were-- who died so-called naturally from fleck typhus.
But these people who were just plain wiped out with the gun.
Who knows?
They were-- they were just probably-- I read about it, that they took over to Budapest about 250 bodies and they tried to-- tried to find out who they are.
I don't think they were very successful, because how can you figure it out?
This was Balf.
Now this-- now we start-- now we are at the beginning of that march, which starts from Balf.
It was Passover time.
And nights were-- the nights were-- the Russian-- Russians used to come down.
They call it Stalingrad candles.
Did you ever hear the word?
Please explain it.
They light up the whole-- in middle of the night, it became so light like it is here.
Huge candles and they were standing in middle of the air.
They call it the Stalingrad candles.
Everything was so light, like-- at least like daytime, so they saw everything.
And they came down and they were bombing the whole Sopron.

They were-- and naturally, the roads were full of the retreating German army, plus the Hungarian civilians, plus-- in other words, the Jewish campers, I don't know whether we were-- what we were over there, [NON-ENGLISH], whatever you call-- call us.
We did not have the road for ourselves.
And we had to go on the side roads through forests.
And we ran through forests, and those German monsters who were chasing us.
And the local Germans were waiting for us, they saw these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] marching Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] groups.
They came out with their-- all their brutality, which you can imagine or not imagine.
They came out with dogs.
They came out with sticks.
They came out with-- with shovels.
Boys and girls, Hitlerjugend.
That was the second night.
I'm-- I'm jumping the gun.
That was two days.
This is the first night was that we-- we went through a forest.
My father-in-law was in the same group.
How do I know it?
Later, many, many years ago, after he was-- he became my father-in-law, he told me, sure, I was there.
The name of the town was Loretto.
It's before-- between-- between Hungarian border and Vienna.
This was the upper Alps.
And we were marching.
And there was some German army officer was there, through the forest.
And there was some little puddle.
The puddle must have been about two-- two yard wide.
And anybody who didn't-- didn't jump over, boom.
Shot him.
They shot him.
It was horrible.
And we had people over there 60.
We had boys 14, and many, many of them.
And they-- they were-- and some places, they were counting one, two, three, gun.
One, two, three, and a bullet.
One, two, three, and a bullet.
And those people who were shot, they were put on the side and they were living.
They were just plain living human beings, who were shot once or twice, but they were still alive.
And the last 25 people were supposed to be-- stopped that they should bury these people.
But these people were not dead yet.
They were just in middle of dying.
This was the first night of-- first night of our march.
The second march-- the second night, I remember that we were just begging each other, this is-- this is a lost game.
This is a-- this is the last trip.
Last-- The next night, we came through a town Loretto.
And I don't know whether-- how to explain it.

But this was a very customary thing among this-- every little village, every little town or city, before the last German army group went through it or trucks, they made barricades into the width of the-- of the street.
Make believe that this is a street.
This is-- this is the width of a street.
So they put in barricades.
But the barricade was they putting in long pieces of poles-- how do you call?-- beams-- beams.
And they left open only so much that a truck should go through.

So the very last group before the-- the last truck will go through, they will fill that in too, in order that it should be-- the highway should be totally blocked, that the Russians shouldn't be able to go through it.
I don't know whether the Russian-- I don't think the Russian tanks were afraid of this, because they could probably go up.
But this was in every town you could see this.
They made a ditch and they filled it in on the left side and on the-- beams, long beams.
And leave open just as much that the marchers and the trucks should go through.
And this Loretto was like this, except that the Germans, what they did, the local people wanted to have fun.
And that little opening, they put on chains in the middle.
That means they were-- they were trying to hold us back.
We were supposed to go, but they put on all type of-- all type of things to hold us back.
We shouldn't be able to go.
So what happened, there was a ditch over there, full of water.
And they were chasing us.
Our-- our guards were chasing us.
All of a sudden, this-- this whole opening became a whole mountain of people, human mountain.
And the German kids, they're just-- just shooting into a mountain like it will be-- like you would see a-- just shooting in.
And it was a huge mountain.
By the time I come-- I came there, I-- I was young enough and I went through it.
But instead of going through-- because we had to go five-- five people together.
This was their style, five people in a row, five people in a row, five people in a row.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] By the time we go through this terrible blockade-- this was actually a blockade.
They wanted us to go through a blockade.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hundreds of people were dead and dying and crying and screaming, screaming, horrible screaming.
And the town was full of boys and girls having fun.
And by the time they came out, I said, my-- my goodness.
How can I-- how-- how am I going to go through the town?
You had-- I am alone walking, because instead of being in five, groups, now you are one at a time.
And I got-- one guy gave me a-- he gave me-- and I told him, I am a young boy.
Leave me alone.
He says, but you're schlepping a big package, don't you?
He gave me a bang with a huge-- it was-- it was like a bamboo stick about two yard long.
Gave me-- I fall down, big bang bump here, bleeding, bleeding.
And I didn't know where I was.
I lost my hat.
I put on my snow hat.
And I lost-- I don't know whether-- no, I picked up my package.
But when I got up, I was-- I was-- I was totally dizzy.
I didn't know which way to go, because the screaming and I didn't see any group to go.
There was no marchers anymore.
